questions.question: Define a computer virus and explain its essential components.
keyPoints: A computer virus is malicious software that attaches to legitimate programs
Requires a host program to propagate
Needs human intervention to spread (user action)
Can corrupt, delete, or steal data
Requires replication mechanism to spread to other files

questions.question: Distinguish between a computer virus and a computer worm.
keyPoints: Virus requires host file; worm is standalone
Virus needs human action to spread; worm spreads automatically
Worms exploit network vulnerabilities
Worms can replicate without user intervention
Both cause damage but differ in propagation method

questions.question: What is a Trojan Horse? List two examples of Trojan types.
keyPoints: Trojan is malware disguised as legitimate software
Does not self-replicate like viruses or worms
Creates backdoors for unauthorized access
Types: Remote Access Trojan (RAT), Keylogger Trojan, Ransomware Trojan, Rootkit Trojan
Delivered through downloads, email attachments, or malicious websites

questions.question: Explain the concept of a botnet and its common uses in cyberattacks.
keyPoints: Bot is a compromised computer controlled remotely
Botnet is a network of such compromised machines (zombies)
Controlled through Command and Control (C2) servers
Used for DDoS attacks, spam distribution, cryptocurrency mining
Can steal data, send phishing emails, and spread other malware

questions.question: What is a polymorphic virus? How does it evade detection?
keyPoints: Polymorphic virus changes its code signature with each infection
Uses encryption with variable keys to alter its appearance
Makes signature-based antivirus detection ineffective
Virus decrypts differently each time it runs
Requires heuristic and behavioral detection methods

questions.question: Explain the working of a boot sector virus.
keyPoints: Infects Master Boot Record (MBR) of hard drives
Executes automatically when computer starts before OS loads
Can infect bootable media like USB drives
Often spreads by copying itself to boot sectors
Difficult to remove as it loads before antivirus software

questions.question: What is a rootkit? Discuss its characteristics.
keyPoints: Rootkit is designed to hide its presence on a system
Conceals other malware from detection
Operates at operating system level (kernel-level)
Modifies system calls and hides processes
Provides continued privileged access to attackers

questions.question: Discuss the different types of viruses. Explain each type with examples.
keyPoints: File Virus: Attaches to executable files (.exe, .com)
Boot Sector Virus: Infects MBR, executes at startup
Macro Virus: Infects document files with macros
Multipartite Virus: Infects both files and boot sectors
Stealth Virus: Uses techniques to hide from antivirus
Polymorphic Virus: Changes code to evade detection
Worm: Self-replicating, spreads via networks

questions.question: Explain the lifecycle of a malware attack. What are the different phases?
keyPoints: Initial Reconnaissance: Gathering information about target
Initial Compromise: Gaining access through vulnerability
Establish Foothold: Installing malware/backdoor
Privilege Escalation: Gaining higher access rights
Lateral Movement: Spreading to other systems
Collection/Exfiltration: Stealing data
Maintenance: Ensuring continued access
Cleanup: Removing evidence of attack

questions.question: Compare and contrast viruses, worms, Trojans, and bots. Provide a tabular comparison.
keyPoints: Virus: Requires host, human intervention, attaches to files
Worm: Self-replicating, no host needed, network-based spread
Trojan: Disguised as legitimate, creates backdoors, no replication
Bot: Compromised system, part of botnet, automated attacks
Virus/Worm: Can self-replicate; Trojan/Bot: Cannot self-replicate
Worm/Bot: Spread automatically; Virus/Trojan: Require user action
All are harmful but differ in propagation and purpose

questions.question: Describe various techniques used by malware to evade detection.
keyPoints: Polymorphism: Changing code signatures
Encryption: Using encrypted payloads
Obfuscation: Hiding code logic
Rootkits: Hiding processes and files
Fileless attacks: Operating in memory only
Living-off-the-land: Using legitimate tools
Anti-debugging: Detecting analysis environments
Time-based triggers: Executing at specific times
